model_output_raw_text: {"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"With a fixed 256-step budget, doubling batch size lets the model see roughly twice as many training examples while keeping the same number of optimizer updates. On full CIFAR-10 with no label noise or imbalance, that extra coverage is a simple high-upside change.","secondary_modes":["topk","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The previous jump in batch size produced a large gain, which suggests this workload is dominated by data coverage under the fixed step budget. Increasing to 256 is a simple follow-up that should expose the model to about a full epoch of data within 256 updates.","secondary_modes":["summaries","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 64","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 48","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"After recovering much more data coverage with larger batches, the next bottleneck is likely model capacity. A modest width increase in both the conv trunk and MLP head keeps the architecture simple while giving the classifier more room to use the extra training signal.","secondary_modes":["micro","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 3","old":"DEPTH = 2","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The model is still very shallow for CIFAR-10. Adding a third conv-pool block should improve feature hierarchy and expand the final channel count while also shrinking the flattened spatial size, making this a clean capacity increase without a large classifier explosion.","secondary_modes":["micro","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The run is still fixed by update count rather than epochs, and successive increases in batch size have consistently helped by improving data coverage. Moving to 512 should let this stronger three-block model see well over one full pass through CIFAR-10 within the same 256 updates.","secondary_modes":["layout","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"Batch size and model capacity have both increased substantially, but the learning rate is still relatively conservative for Adam under a short cosine schedule. Raising it is the simplest way to trade the remaining fixed-step budget for faster optimization.","secondary_modes":["micro","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The current setup now uses a fairly large batch, a wider deeper model, and a higher learning rate, which makes decoupled weight decay more attractive than classic Adam with L2-style decay. Switching to AdamW is a simple optimizer-family refinement with good odds of improving validation loss without changing the rest of the training loop.","secondary_modes":["caching","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 24","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Most recent optimizer changes are near saturation, while earlier capacity increases produced strong gains. Raising the stem width keeps the architecture simple and should give the three-block network substantially more representational power without changing the training logic.","secondary_modes":["micro","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 128","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The widened convolutional trunk is now much stronger, but the MLP head is still relatively narrow. Increasing the hidden size is a cheap capacity boost compared with enlarging the conv stack further, so it is a good next layout change given the already long runtime.","secondary_modes":["micro","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 3e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The model has grown substantially in both convolutional and classifier capacity, and the latest gains from adding head width were smaller. A modest increase in decoupled weight decay is a simple way to improve generalization without adding runtime or architectural complexity.","secondary_modes":["indexing","layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 3e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.1","old":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.0","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The stronger weight decay was slightly harmful, so this candidate backs it out and tests a gentler form of regularization. With the wider trunk and larger MLP head, a small dropout rate may improve validation loss without constraining optimization as much as heavier decay.","secondary_modes":["layout","indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.0","old":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.1","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"Dropout was clearly too disruptive under this short training budget, so this candidate removes it and then makes a small upward LR adjustment. The current large-batch AdamW setup is still likely optimization-limited enough that a modest step-size increase is worth testing.","secondary_modes":["caching","indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 3.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The last learning-rate increase was beneficial, suggesting the current large-batch AdamW configuration still tolerates a more aggressive step size. A modest follow-up increase is the simplest high-upside continuation before changing the training structure again.","secondary_modes":["indexing","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"summaries","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    warmup_steps = min(8, max(1, total_steps // 10))","old":"    warmup_steps = min(16, max(1, total_steps // 10))","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"summaries","rationale":"The model now benefits from a fairly aggressive learning rate, so spending 16 of only 256 steps in warmup may be unnecessarily conservative. Shortening warmup is a minimal schedule change that preserves the cosine decay shape while letting the run reach its effective step size earlier.","secondary_modes":["topk","indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    warmup_steps = min(16, max(1, total_steps // 10))","old":"    warmup_steps = min(8, max(1, total_steps // 10))","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 256","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 128","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The shorter warmup was harmful, so this candidate restores the better schedule and spends the remaining complexity budget on a larger classifier head. The convolutional trunk is now strong and wide, and a bigger MLP head is a relatively cheap way to improve how those features are separated.","secondary_modes":["summaries","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 28","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 24","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Capacity increases have been the most reliable source of gains on this workload, and the current model is still structurally simple. A modest bump in the base channel width is the next clean way to expand representational power without changing the training loop or schedule.","secondary_modes":["topk","micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 32","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 28","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Width increases have continued to pay off, and the architecture remains simple and stable. With only a few steps left, another modest bump in base channels is the most direct way to keep pushing representational power upward.","secondary_modes":["topk","micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 384","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The current widened trunk is working well, and increasing classifier width has previously produced gains at much lower runtime cost than another large convolutional expansion. With only a couple of trials left, enlarging the MLP head is a pragmatic way to probe remaining capacity headroom.","secondary_modes":["topk","micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 512","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 384","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Recent classifier-width increases have produced consistent gains and are cheaper than another full convolutional widening. For the final trial, expanding the MLP head is the most pragmatic way to probe remaining capacity without perturbing the now-strong optimizer and schedule settings.","secondary_modes":["topk","micro"]}
